# UIcockpit

> Open-source, browser-based design system configurator for developers and designers building modern web apps. Pick a Style and a color theme or customize from scratch — UIcockpit produces framework-neutral design tokens, component CSS recipes, and an agent pack (spec + skill rules + a checkable contract) that tells AI coding tools how to use them. It is the design-language layer that sits above component primitives like shadcn/ui, so AI-built apps don't all look generic.

URL: https://uicockpit.com/
License: MIT
Price: Free
Account required: No
Telemetry: None

## What it is

UIcockpit is a single-page web app that helps a developer or product designer build a coherent design system in under a minute. About two dozen visual controls (covering color, typography, radii, motion, density, icons, surface backgrounds, state layers) re-render a full component gallery and live showcases as the user tweaks. You can start from scratch — or **start from your existing app**: paste its CSS / Tailwind / HTML, or drop a screenshot, and UIcockpit reads back its foundation (colors, type, radius, density, dark-mode) into editable controls. The output is an install-ready kit: a hosted token link plus an agent pack that tells an AI coding tool exactly how to apply it.

## Who it's for

The "vibe-coder" audience: developers who build with AI tools (Cursor, v0, Lovable, Claude Code, Bolt) using shadcn/ui and Tailwind v4 as their default stack. UIcockpit fills the "I need a design system before I start coding" gap that AI tools leave open — generated components otherwise default to plain shadcn defaults regardless of the project's brand identity.

## What you get when you export

Export is framed as an install experience, not a format dump — a hosted link plus a downloadable agent pack:

**Quick install**

1. **Hosted kit link** — your whole kit lives at a CDN URL (`https://kit.uicockpit.com/k/<hash>.css`). Link it from any app and the app wears the kit — no build step, no files to vendor, edit the kit and the link updates.

2. **The agent pack** (one download / zip):
   - **design.md** — the human + AI spec: plain-English decisions, the full token list, a complete WCAG audit (16 contrast pairs), do's and don'ts, and a composition contract telling an agent which recipe owns which job.
   - **An agent skill file** — short imperative always/never rules an AI tool loads every session, delivered where your tool expects it (Claude `SKILL.md`, Cursor `.cursor/rules/uicockpit.mdc`, Windsurf `.windsurfrules`, or a generic `AGENTS.md`).
   - **contract.json** — the machine-readable contract a verifier checks the agent's output against (`uicockpit check`): it catches hardcoded hex, off-grid spacing, magic px and wrong tokens. The write → check → fix loop is what actually binds an agent, not just nudges it.

**Other formats** (eject to raw files)

3. **tokens.css** — `:root` + `.dark` blocks with 120+ CSS custom properties. Drop-in. Includes a `@font-face`/Google Fonts import, full motion system (durations, easings, named animations), state tokens (disabled, focus ring, validation), z-index stack, breakpoints, container widths, chart palette, plus reusable component CSS recipes (Button, Input, Card, Badge, Tabs, Dialog, Menu, Tooltip, Avatar, Progress, Skeleton, Spinner, Kbd, Separator, etc.)

4. **Tailwind v4 globals.css** — `@theme` block ready to drop into Next.js/Vite projects. Every utility (`bg-primary`, `text-muted`, `shadow-md`, `font-display`, etc.) resolves to the configured value.

5. **shadcn/ui globals.css** — the standard shadcn CSS variable contract (`--background`, `--primary`, `--ring`, etc.) so shadcn components inherit the kit without modification.

The kit's decisions, the full WCAG audit, and the agent rules all live in **design.md** (the old BRIEF.md + AI-prompt, merged). A W3C `tokens.json` (Tokens Studio / Figma) is available on request but no longer a default tab.

## Distinctive features vs. competitors

- **Framework-neutral output**: not coupled to any one stack. Same export works in Next.js, Vite + React, SvelteKit, Astro, vanilla CSS.
- **WCAG audit baked in**: 16 contrast pairs tested live; pass count visible in the topbar. Failing pairs include remedy hints.
- **Component recipes shipped**: most token systems only give variables — UIcockpit also ships the per-component CSS (hover, focus, disabled, active states) so AI tools and designers don't have to invent them.
- **Auto-derived chart palette**: 6 series colors from the active theme. No more `chart-1: red, chart-2: green, ...` random defaults.
- **No accounts, URL-hash save**: a configuration link is a kit. Paste in Slack/Discord/Email, the recipient sees the exact kit.
- **Two orthogonal starting axes** (not brand clones): pick a **Style** — the form/shape language (radius, density, motion + elevation signature) — and cross it with a **Color theme** (10 curated brand hues: cobalt, sky, teal, jade, indigo, violet, coral, rose, ember, mono). Then tweak any of the controls. The result is your own design language, not a copy of someone else's app.

## How UIcockpit compares to other tools

UIcockpit is not a component library — it is the design-language / token layer that sits above one. Honest positioning vs adjacent tools:

- **vs shadcn/ui** — shadcn gives you component source you own; UIcockpit gives you the framework-neutral design language on top, and even exports a drop-in shadcn globals.css. Complementary, not rivals. https://uicockpit.com/compare/shadcn
- **vs v0 (Vercel)** — v0 generates one-off UI from prompts; UIcockpit produces a consistent, reusable design system across every screen, and its AI prompt keeps v0's output on-brand. https://uicockpit.com/compare/v0
- **vs TweakCN** — TweakCN is a focused shadcn theme editor; UIcockpit is a broader, framework-neutral system (type, density, motion, recipes, a hosted kit link + an agent pack). https://uicockpit.com/compare/tweakcn
- **vs Material UI (MUI)** — MUI is a mature React component library with the Material Design look; UIcockpit emits framework-neutral, zero-runtime tokens for your own aesthetic on any stack. https://uicockpit.com/compare/material-ui
- **vs Tailwind UI** — Tailwind UI sells ready-made blocks; UIcockpit is the free design system that themes them. https://uicockpit.com/compare/tailwind-ui

Use-case landing pages: design tokens for Tailwind v4 (https://uicockpit.com/uses/design-tokens-for-tailwind), shadcn theme generator (https://uicockpit.com/uses/shadcn-theme-generator), AI design system (https://uicockpit.com/uses/ai-design-system), framework-neutral design tokens (https://uicockpit.com/uses/framework-neutral-design-tokens).

## Tech stack

- Vite 8 + React 19 + TypeScript strict
- No backend, no database, no telemetry
- Static deployable to any CDN (Cloudflare Pages, Vercel, Netlify)
- PWA with service-worker caching
- 120+ design tokens computed via in-browser color engine
- 5 icon libraries (Lucide, Iconoir, Phosphor regular/bold, Heroicons solid), dynamically imported

## Common questions

**Q: Do I need to know design to use UIcockpit?**
A: No. Start from a Style + a Color theme, optionally tweak a few sliders, then export. The defaults pass WCAG AA contrast.

**Q: Can I edit the exported tokens later?**
A: Yes — the export is standard CSS / JSON. Open in any editor. If you want to start from where you left off, just paste the original UIcockpit URL.

**Q: Does it work with my existing app?**
A: If your app uses Tailwind v4 or shadcn/ui, drop the export in and rebuild. If it uses plain CSS, drop tokens.css in and reference the variables. Nothing in UIcockpit's output assumes a specific framework beyond standard CSS custom properties.

**Q: Is the WCAG audit reliable?**
A: It tests 16 actual color pairs from your kit (text-on-bg, button text on solid fills, status colors on tints, input borders, focus rings) and reports the contrast ratios with pass/fail vs. WCAG AA. Decorative borders are excluded (per WCAG 1.4.11 spec) — only functional UI elements are measured.

**Q: Why no account system?**
A: The URL hash IS your save format. Every kit is shareable as a link. Custom fonts uploaded via drag-and-drop stay in the browser session and are never sent to any server. This makes UIcockpit zero-trust by design.
